Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings, but it never really stuck as a habit. I learn best by listening, so I'd often put on lecture recordings while doing other things around my apartment. Some weeks I'd manage 25 hours, other weeks barely anything. I kept telling myself I'd get more organized, but life just kept getting in the way.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these bursts of activity right before deadlines, then nothing for days. When I didn't have immediate pressure, I'd drift and focus on other things. It wasn't intentional—I just struggled to maintain consistency without external pressure pushing me forward.
Q3: Usually it was a notification about an upcoming deadline or someone from my peer support group messaging me about an assignment. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important. The anxiety of potentially falling behind would hit me randomly, and I'd suddenly feel compelled to check the LMS even if I didn't end up doing much once I was there.
Q4: For a brief period, checking the LMS in the morning became somewhat routine after my peer support group started doing weekly check-ins. We'd message each other about what we were working on, and that accountability helped. But honestly, it never became truly automatic for me—it always felt like something I had to consciously decide to do rather than a natural habit.
Q5: The recorded lectures were my lifeline since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times and actually absorb the information better than reading. The discussion boards were also helpful because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. My peer support group connections through the LMS genuinely helped me feel less isolated.
Q6: I barely touched the supplementary reading materials and PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on screen was exhausting. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The optional practice quizzes also got ignored because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to an assignment or exam. If I could listen to it rather than read it, even better. Anything that felt abstract or disconnected from what I actually needed to submit just got pushed aside. I needed to feel like my limited energy was being spent on something that would directly help my grade.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required work already took everything I had, so optional stuff became invisible to me. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but the anxiety of missing required deadlines was stronger. Required work had consequences I could see; optional work just felt like it was for students who had their lives together.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on my phone during my commute or while doing chores. I also borrowed textbooks from the library since I couldn't afford to buy them, and I'd study from those without being online at all. Sometimes I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play those recordings back—none of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be listening to downloaded lectures while walking or cooking, genuinely processing the material, but the LMS had no idea. I also had study sessions with people from my peer support group where we'd talk through concepts for hours. Those conversations were some of my most valuable learning moments, but they happened over coffee, not on the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it maybe three or four more times offline over the following week. Same with any slides that were available—I'd save them to my phone and review them on the bus. The LMS only saw that initial download, not the repeated engagement that happened afterward.
Q12: There were weeks when my anxiety got really bad, and even though I'm generally resilient, it became hard to start anything. I'd open the LMS, see all the things I hadn't done, feel overwhelmed, and close it again. Financial stress was also a factor—when you're worried about rent, coursework feels less urgent. Those periods showed up as gaps in my activity.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working extra hours to cover bills, the LMS became this thing I'd just stare at without really processing anything. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting them. Competing responsibilities meant I'd often choose sleep or work over logging in. The fatigue was real, and it showed in my inconsistent engagement.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about money, thinking about other problems. I'd spend an hour technically 'engaged' but retain almost nothing. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like this huge wall I couldn't climb. I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just scared of doing it wrong. The peer support group helped sometimes, but often I'd still wait until the last possible moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the 48 hours before something was due. Without that pressure, I'd drift. It wasn't healthy, but that urgency was the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and actually get me to produce work. My whole engagement pattern was deadline-shaped.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback surprised me and gave me a temporary boost of motivation. Critical feedback, even when constructive, would sometimes make me avoid that course for a few days while I processed it. I appreciated feedback intellectually, but emotionally it could be hard to handle, which affected when I'd re-engage.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming at times because there was so much happening across four online courses simultaneously. When modules were clearly organized and I could see exactly what was expected, I engaged more. But when things felt scattered or the expectations were unclear, I'd freeze up and avoid the whole thing until panic set in.
Q19: Clear audio content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when navigation was confusing. Having peer support integrated helped because I could reach out when stuck. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it easy to lose track.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—anxious, trying to figure things out, opening everything to see what's there but getting overwhelmed before actually completing anything. High activity can be a sign of struggling, not success. You're searching for something that will make it click, but you can't find it, so you keep clicking around without producing output.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. Some of my best learning happened offline—listening to downloaded content, talking with peers, studying from library books. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity wasn't impressive. The platform only captures one slice of how learning actually happens. Real understanding doesn't require constant logging in.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they stay. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm anxiously scrolling without absorbing anything or deeply focused while offline. They miss the conversations, the thinking that happens away from the screen, the emotional state behind the clicks.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just getting through it. It's when I'm genuinely curious and thinking about concepts even when I'm not studying. For me, real engagement often felt internal and invisible—those moments when something finally clicked during a conversation or while listening to a lecture for the third time.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels, my financial stress, or how hard I was actually trying despite inconsistent activity. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that kept me from dropping out, or the offline studying that contributed to my decent exam scores. It sees the surface of my behavior, not the struggle or resilience underneath it.
